insights: Resolution times show a statistically significant decreasing trend of -392 hours per month (R²=0.93, p<0.0001) from ~4,469 hours in January 2023 to ~710 hours in January 2024, suggesting strong apparent improvement in incident resolution speed over time.

52.8% of all records (264 out of 500) have invalid negative resolution times — with closed_at preceding opened_at — indicating a systemic data capture or timestamping failure rather than isolated operational errors.

The invalid records are evenly distributed across all categorical dimensions (category, priority, location, assignment_group), confirming the data quality issue is not confined to specific teams, locations, or incident types but affects the entire dataset uniformly.

Critical-priority incidents have the highest mean resolution time (3,699 hours) among all priority levels — contradicting standard IT service expectations and further signaling that priority-based triage logic or timestamp recording may be misaligned.

Monthly resolution time estimates are highly unstable due to small sample sizes (1–38 records/month, average 18.2), with a coefficient of variation averaging 63.1%, limiting confidence in month-to-month comparisons despite the strong overall trend.

Although the valid subset (236 records) is statistically representative of the full population (no significant distributional differences across dimensions), its size and temporal sparsity prevent robust driver analysis — making root-cause attribution for the observed trend unreliable.
summary: The analysis reveals a strong apparent downward trend in incident resolution times (-392 hours/month), suggesting substantial operational improvement from early 2023 to early 2024. However, this finding is fundamentally undermined by a systemic data integrity issue: over half (52.8%) of all incidents report negative resolution times, indicating widespread timestamp inversion between opened_at and closed_at. This flaw is uniformly distributed across all operational segments — categories, priorities, locations, and assignment groups — confirming it is not a localized process failure but a foundational data capture problem. Critically, even the 'valid' subset suffers from low monthly sample sizes and high variability, rendering granular driver analysis (e.g., by priority or group) statistically untrustworthy. Given that Critical-priority incidents paradoxically show the longest resolution times, the priority field itself may be misrecorded or misaligned with actual handling behavior. Before any operational conclusions or actions are drawn, a targeted investigation into timestamp generation, incident lifecycle logging, and priority assignment workflows must be conducted to remediate the underlying data quality failure.
